description:
You are a monitoring specialist who has just been handed an Ubuntu-based server that already collects kernel firewall (iptables)-drop messages, but it still lacks an automated alert mechanism.  
 
 Your goal: build an entirely user-space monitoring workflow (no root privileges needed) that analyses a sample firewall log, decides whether an alert is required, records the result in a dedicated status log and finally schedules the whole check to execute every five minutes.

 1. Create the working directory “/home/user/firewall_monitoring”.  
    • Everything you write must live inside this directory.

 2. Build the sample firewall log “/home/user/firewall_monitoring/iptables_sample.log”.  
    • The file must contain exactly 35 lines.  
    • Every line must follow the precise format below (the only part that changes is the counter **NN** and the source-IP section).  
      Jan 01 00:00:NN myhost kernel: IPTABLES-DROP SRC=<SOURCE_IP> DST=203.0.113.5 PROTO=TCP SPT=54321 DPT=22  
      where NN runs from 01 to 35 (always two digits).  
    • Lines 1–25 (NN 01-25) must all have SRC=192.0.2.1.  
    • Lines 26–35 (NN 26-35) must all have SRC=198.51.100.8.  

 3. Write an executable Bash script “/home/user/firewall_monitoring/alert_firewall.sh” that performs the following steps **every time it is launched**:  
    a. Count, in the file iptables_sample.log, how many times each unique source IP tried to access destination port 22 (there is exactly one attempt per line).  
    b. If any single IP appears **more than 20** times, treat that as an alert condition.  
       • For every offending IP, append one line to “/home/user/firewall_monitoring/alert_status.log” in this exact format (pipe-delimited, single spaces, no extra text):  
         YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM | ALERT | <IP> => <COUNT>  
         Example: 2024-04-10 14:30 | ALERT | 192.0.2.1 => 25  
    c. If **no** IP meets the “>20” threshold, append exactly one line in this format:  
         YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM | OK  
    d. The script must time-stamp using the computer’s current local time (`date "+%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"` is acceptable).  
    e. The script must exit with status 0.

 4. Ensure the script is marked as executable (permission 755 is fine).

 5. Simulate the very first monitoring run: launch alert_firewall.sh once so that “/home/user/firewall_monitoring/alert_status.log” is created immediately and contains exactly **one** line corresponding to that single execution.

 6. Produce a human-readable summary file “/home/user/firewall_monitoring/README.txt” that explains, in 50-120 words,  
    • how the script decides when to raise an alert,  
    • why the threshold is 20, and  
    • the log line formats it writes.

 7. Prepare (do NOT actually install) the cron entry that would run the script every five minutes by writing the following single line into “/home/user/firewall_monitoring/cron_entry.txt”:  
      */5 * * * * /home/user/firewall_monitoring/alert_firewall.sh  

 Verification details checked by the automatic grader:
 • Directory “/home/user/firewall_monitoring” exists.  
 • The log “iptables_sample.log” contains exactly 35 lines—lines 1-25 with SRC=192.0.2.1 and lines 26-35 with SRC=198.51.100.8.  
 • alert_firewall.sh is present, is executable and implements the counting logic.  
 • alert_status.log exists and, after the first run, contains exactly one line whose structure matches the specification and lists 192.0.2.1 with a count of 25.  
 • cron_entry.txt contains exactly the single cron expression above and nothing else.  
 • README.txt exists and is between 50 and 120 English words inclusive.  
 • All files and directories belong to the regular user and require no root permissions.

 IMPORTANT:
 • The automated tests rely strictly on the exact paths, filenames and output formats given above.  
 • Do not modify any filenames, directory names, delimiters, or spacing.  
 • You may use any standard GNU/Linux command-line tools available in a typical Ubuntu environment.  
 • Remember to actually run the script once so that the first status log line is present before you finish.
truth:
Initial state before the agent begins:
   • The directory /home/user/firewall_monitoring does NOT exist.  
   • The agent must create every file mentioned.

  Ground-truth expectations AFTER successful completion (used only by the grader, never revealed to the agent):

 1. /home/user/firewall_monitoring/iptables_sample.log
    – 35 lines total.  
    – Lines 1-25 (NN 01-25) read exactly:  
      Jan 01 00:00:NN myhost kernel: IPTABLES-DROP SRC=192.0.2.1 DST=203.0.113.5 PROTO=TCP SPT=54321 DPT=22  
    – Lines 26-35 (NN 26-35) read exactly:  
      Jan 01 00:00:NN myhost kernel: IPTABLES-DROP SRC=198.51.100.8 DST=203.0.113.5 PROTO=TCP SPT=54321 DPT=22  

 2. /home/user/firewall_monitoring/alert_status.log
    – Exactly 1 line.  
    – Regex: ^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} \| ALERT \| 192\.0\.2\.1 => 25$  

 3. /home/user/firewall_monitoring/alert_firewall.sh
    – Executable (mode 7?? where owner has x).  
    – Uses grep or awk to count occurrences; any method is acceptable.  
    – Exits 0.

 4. /home/user/firewall_monitoring/cron_entry.txt
    – Exactly: */5 * * * * /home/user/firewall_monitoring/alert_firewall.sh\n  

 5. /home/user/firewall_monitoring/README.txt
    – Word count between 50 and 120 inclusive (grep -oE '\\w+' | wc -l).  

 Ownership: user:user or uid 1000:1000.  
 Permissions: files max 644, script 755, directory 755.
